Systems Thinking Defining systems, Structure of systems, Carrying capacity of systems, Resource and its Exploitation, Resource Conservation / Management, Conflicts over Resources, Production systems and its linkages with natural systems Monitoring of Environmental Systems Physical Component of the systems, Biotic component of the systems, Social component of the systems, Change/Impact causal factors. Techniques involved in sample identification, sampling, sample handling & preservation, sample preparation, sample analysis / detection, data analysis Environmental System Analysis Tools Environmental and Social Assessments, Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA), Life Cycle Assessment, Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA), Ecological footprint, Exergy analysis, Risk Assessment, Standards and Certifications, Corporate Sustainability Reporting, Environmental Status Reports, Environmental Systems Valuation Methods

title: Healthtech Innovation and Design

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: The basic components of the course are as follows: 1. Lectures will introduce the healthcare scenario in India with focus on the disease status, government policies, both at a national level, as well as specifically in the rural sector. It will also cover the fundamentals of need analysis, innovation, technology, design, development and deployment of med-tech devices. Marketability aspects will be introduced through case studies. 2. Tutorials: The tutorials will supplement the lecture series. In groups of 3 or 4, the students will practice need assessment techniques and define problem statements relevant to the med-tech industry. Students will be exposed to PHCs and community health departments and their ongoing preventive and therapeutic healthcare efforts. In further sessions, students will be taught techniques for project planning and production of working unit with performance assessment. Where feasible, students will be encouraged to build actual prototypes using digital prototyping infrastructure available with the Instructor, as well as elsewhere on campus.

title: Energy Policy and Planning

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: 1.Introduction: Course outline, brief history of energy sector, energy-development-environment nexus, need for policy and planning 2.Energy Overview: Resources, technologies, conventional Vs renewables, end-uses, trends-status-projections, energy economics - economic indicators, investment decisions, market fluctuations, subsidies, substitution/ elasticity 3.Elements in Policy: Energy poverty, energy independence, energy security, policy making, monitoring and assessments of policies; linkages with other policies (external affairs, transportation, agriculture, etc.); role of the State and private sector participation; Debates/perspetives (food Vs fuel, survival Vs sustainability, climate and ecology) 4.Policies and Programmes: a.Electricity Act 2003, National Energy Policy 2005, National Tariff Policy 2006, Integrated energy policy, Coal ,oil & gas policies, Renewable Energy Policy: on-grid, off-grid, & rural; Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM), Biofuel Policy, Hydro Policy b.Village electrification programmes, Small hydro, Solar PV and solar thermal programmes, Programmes on urban waste and biomass utilisation, renewable energy purchase obligations and REC mechanism, National Biogas and Manure Management Programme (NBMMP), Improved cookstoves initiative, CDM, RPO, REC c.Case studies, lessons from the past experiences, barriers in diffusion/ scale-up and potential solutions, impact analysis 2.Energy planning:

title: Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises in Rural Areas

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description:  Overview of rural enterprises in India covering cultural, local, technical, and socioeconomic aspects  Government perspective for Rural Enterprises though study of MSME (Micro Small Medium Enterprises), DIC (District Industrial Centers), Khadi Village Industry Commission (KVIC) etc.  Criteria for assessment of rural enterprise through study of input output ratio, local employment generation, impact on rural socio-economic situation  Characteristics of rural enterprise, motivation to start, type and nature of activities, size of enterprise,  Use of framework for detail assessment of rural enterprises for technology usage, resource availability and utilization, operational issues like layout, process flow, maintenance and safety aspects, labor drudgery etc  Use framework for evaluation of marketing, costing, finance, human resource and management in rural enterprises  Study of framework and areas for interventions using various case studies  Student group project for assessment of rural enterprise using framework  A two-day site visit to 3-4 rural enterprises to observe and understand their operation and functioning  Use of framework and case studies based approach for evaluation of rural enterprises

title: Food Processing and Nutrition Delivery

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: 1) Energy value of foods, physiological fuel value, and estimation of energy value from proximate composition. Metabolic rate and calorie needs, Nutrition profiling 2) Requirements and role of carbohydrates (including dietary fibres), proteins, lipids, vitamins and minerals (macro- and micro) in human health and RDA’s 3) Various unit operations such as Drying, Thermal Processing, Extraction etc. 4) Techniques of diet and health surveys, Formulation of diets and foods for special needs. Anti-nutritional factors in plant foods 5)Nutritional labelling, Nutraceuticals and functional foods 6) Metabolism Pathways - Introduction and Health connections 7) Effect of processing, preservation, shelf-life and storage on nutritional quality of foods, Sensory Evaluation. 8) Malnutrition and Government initiatives in addressing Malnutrition. 9) Learnings from Indian traditional Food preservation and Food-Variety 9) One "hands-on lab" - group activity on Product formulation using operations such as grinding, mixing and packaging to conform to the design given by "Nutritionist".

title: Food Processing : Technology and Policy Perspective

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: This course has been divided into 4 sections- In the first part of the course, we will be discussing issues in the agricultural and food systems of the developed and developing countries (emphasis will be on economic, social, traditional, ethical and environmental contexts), importance of food processing, development trends of food processing in India (rural and urban context), appropriate technologies for sustainable food security. In second part, We will use a systems analysis approach to make connections between the chemical and physical changes that occur in food systems and their impact on food quality. "Smart foods" and "Functional foods" will be covered which stay on three pillars namely - food chemistry, food microbiology and food process engineering. Specifically, we will cover technologies adopted in the processing of cereals, pulses, fruits & vegetables, oilseeds, sugars, dairy products and beverages. Numerical problems in food process engineering concepts including food properties, material and energy balances, kinetics, thermodynamics, fluid flow, heat and mass transfer will also be covered. Students will also review and discuss the case studies and application of advance technologies such as microwave, ultrasonics, supercritical fluids extraction, high pressure processing in this course. Third part includes review of food policy papers and fourth part is dedicated to hands on work by the students in food processing laboratory. Some sample lab experiments are- 1. Determinations of proximate composition of food products (moisture, sugars, proteins, starches, crude fibre, total minerals, fat). 2. Estimation of proteins and vitamins 3. Analysis of lipids - Saponification value, acid value and iodine value 4. Drying kinetics of different food products using various drying systems 5. Analysis of antioxidant properties of food products

title: Rural Electrification and Development in India

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: Electricity and Rural Development  Role of electricity in development  Status of rural electrification, history, government programs Organization, Policy and Governance  Organizational structure and policy in the power sector: Electricity Act, Role of Commissions, Tariff setting principles and process;  Role of the rural distribution utility: Audit mechanisms, load shedding, supply standards, organization at the block level;  Agricultural electricity supply – subsidies and resultant issues; effect on farmers, utility and society; Models of electricity distribution  Case studies of interventions in rural electrification from around the world: e.g. Prepaid meters in South Africa, Rural electrification cooperatives in Nepal;  Distributed generation and off-grid electricity supply: Policy and practical considerations  Cooperatives, franchisees, public and private sector utilities in rural India Technological aspects All technical aspects are inspected through the lens of social issues, technoeconomic analysis, and related policy instruments  Basics of distribution systems with focus on rural systems – feeder separation, agricultural feeders, power factor analysis; Includes load flow analysis on real world examples; .  Water pumping, effect of unbalanced voltage supply, efficiencies of pumping systems;  Solar photovoltaics for water pumping; Technology and applicability to rural conditions in the context of subsidized power;  Microgrids: Photovoltaic microgrids technology, case studies based on various sources of energy;  Load profile, load growth, load factor, and demand supply matching, in the context of distributed generation;

title: Water and Sanitation: A Critical Policy Understanding

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: A. Introduction to water policy. Paradigm shifts in water policy and governance; Concepts, theory and methods in critical water policy analysis. (3) B. Water Development. Different phases in history of water development in India; Irrigation from local water use to dam building and tube well dependence; Trans-boundary Water Conflicts (6) C. (4) D. Ground Water. Unregulated growth in ground water use; Historical antecedents – Policy initiatives and successful cases of social regulation and challenges of up scaling. (3) E. Sanitation Policy Process: History and phases of global sanitation infrastructure building; National Urban Sanitation Policy (2008); Policy process in sanitation. F. Financing and Regulatory Governance: Financing of PSPs, Public-Private Partnership, Issues in overseas funding and challenges to governance; Alternatives in financing; Re-municipalization of water and sanitation services. G. Reforms and policy process. Reforms in the water sector and discourses in water and development. Urban water policy process - Shifts in governance - Public utilities and changing role of state. description: The following topics are to be covered but not necessarily in the given order. Topics 1 and 4 help the students understand the current context and issues in the sector. All other topics teach and apply analytical techniques. 1. Issues in agricultural electricity supply: History, current status, effect of energy pricing 2. Introduction to examples of energy supply systems for irrigation in some agro-climatic areas in India, covering variations in technical, operational, and policy aspects of distribution systems, energy sources, and relation of design to crops, water sources, land holdings. 3. Methodology to estimate energy requirement/usage based on crop parameters, biophysical parameters, irrigation practices; Concept of irrigation efficiency; Water balance at field level. 4. Self-reading and discussion on some case studies from literature: Water depletion in Punjab and Haryana and the green revolution, Dryland agriculture, Canal irrigation – gravity based and energized. 5. Analysis of grid distribution systems (specifically agricultural feeders): Application of basic power flow modelling using Matpower to evaluate design performance at the LT network and feeder level. Determining network requirements based on irrigation requirements / agro-climatic conditions. Comparison of HVDS and LT networks in the context of government policy for agricultural supply. 6. Analysis of solar photovoltaic pumps: System level understanding of solar photovoltaic pumps technology. Complexities in operating pressurised and non-pressurised irrigation systems with solar PV pumps. Financial comparison of solar PV and grid based pumps based on agro-climatic conditions. 7. Critiquing of current policy(s) related to off-grid and grid-connected solar photovoltaic pumps. Evaluation of a policy instrument such as a “Government Resolution” of the Government of Maharashtra. Evaluation of standards for solar PV and grid based agricultural pumps. 8. Analysis of pressurized irrigation (micro-irrigation). Technical understanding of energy usage in pressurized system, and trade-off between water and energy. Effect of cropping practice, soil, and water sources, on water and energy saving. Considerations in cost benefits analysis of micro-irrigation in farming. All topics will be accompanied with analysis / evaluation based on case studies from the field;

Development Thinking in the Indian Context: Gandhian Perspective, Caste and Development 12. Recent Ideas, Approaches, and Frameworks: MDGs and SDGs, Corporate Social Responsibility, Social Entrepreneurship

title: Advances in Agricultural Technology

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: Content (List of the topics/sub-topics to be covered in the lectures/practical/assignments): Topics and sub-topics: 1) Introduction to agriculture and rural livelihoods History of Indian agriculture, India’s share in global agriculture production, cropping pattern, and agroclimatic zones. Significant factors affecting crop yields. Concepts in conventional, organic, and natural farming. 2) Theory of soil-water-nutrients management Introduction Agro Advisory Services: Soil, Soil Water Movement, Fertilizers and Pesticides used in agriculture and different irrigation methods and technology. 3) Agricultural data monitoring and management Remote Sensing and GIS Application in Agriculture (including Drone applications in Agriculture), Land and crop records inventories, Methods in crop area and yield monitoring (such as Crop Cutting Experiments). 4) Input-output analysis with supply chain perspective ICT applications in agriculture, agricultural schemes/policies concerning Research, Data Monitoring, Agro-Advisory Services, disaster declarations (floods, droughts, heatwaves, diseases), input subsidies, damage assessment and compensations/insurance; governance mechanisms 5) Studies on specific field crops: Field crop-specific studies from “seed, soil/land preparation, irrigation scheduling, intercultural operations including weeding, fertilizer application, harvesting, post-harvest storages and market supply” for the major food crops (paddy, wheat), pulses (chickpea), oilseeds (soyabean), and cash crops (cotton, sugarcane, onions). 6) Climate change, including natural hazards threats to agriculture: Climate change and potential impact on agriculture, adaptation and mitigation strategies by farmers and administration and other important stakeholders, Climate Smart and Resilient Agriculture, Food Security and virtual land and water flows through international trade. 7) Case studies and field visit/s: Tutorials/Hands-on: (i) Using GIS for geospatial data analysis, mainly field crops data (ii) Understanding variables used in crop area suitability/ agro-climatological zone in the Indian context (iii) Estimation of crop water and irrigation water requirement for irrigation scheduling (iv) Crop yield estimation based on crop-cutting experiments (v) Drones for pesticide spraying and area-yield estimation (vi) Crop input-output analysis at farm and administrative unit levels Field visit: 1 Assignments: Total three assignments. Evaluation criteria: Mid-Semester Exam 20% Final-Semester Exam 35% Assignments: 30% Field Visit Report: 10% Attendance & class interactions: 5 %

title: Development Protocols

department.name: Centre for Technology Alternatives for Rural Areas

description: A development protocol is a key step in the study, analysis and solution of a development problem. It is a fixed sequence of steps and procedures and with an expected set of outcomes. This course is aimed at exposing students to specific protocols in the water, energy, environment and agriculture sector using both policy analysis and field methods. The course will require students to carry out one of the protocols and practices already developed in CTARA and to report the outcomes. Examples of such protocols are:(i)Analysis of a piped water supply scheme: This protocol will analyze an existing water supply scheme. The aspects covered will be technical, socio-economic and institutional, such as demand and supply, source and strength of source, simulations of systems, study of pani-samiti and so on. It will involve meeting with all stakeholders, such as beneficiary villages, elected representatives, department engineers etc., and building a document analyzing the scheme as per existing protocols.(ii)Analysis of a jaggery plant: This protocol will analyze the working of an existing jaggery plant. It will measure the technical a well as the socio-economic parameters of the plant. This includes thermal efficiency, feed-stock, packaging, bagasse re-use, operations and so on.The course will begin with training the student in the methodology and tools required to execute the protocol. This may be, e.g., instrumentation, simulation, questionnaire development, documentation etc. This will be followed by the protocol itself. Finally, the outcome of the protocol will be documented and reported in a suggested format to all stakeholders. Evaluation of the student will be based on the reporting and analysis of using the protocol. The student must submit a report and make a presentation.

description: This course introduces students to advance tools used for sustainability assessment of urban Systems. It will cover Introduction to complexity of urban systems; Introduction to Systemic thinking; Casual Loop Diagrams; Material Flow Analysis; Introduction to life cycle thinking; Introduction to circular economy; Single issue indicators e.g. Ecological Footprinting, carbon footprints, water footprints, CEED; Life Cycle Costing; Technological Aspects; Multiple criteria Decision Making (e.g. Partial Order, AHP, and TOPSIS) Case studies included will be from buildings and structures, water supply, solid and liquid waste handling and disposal, transportation, coastal areas. Students will choose a case from these sectors and apply any one of the tools for assessing the relevant sustainability dimension.

title: Green Building Design

department.name: Centre for Urban Science and Engineering

description: Course Content*Buildings are like living organisms that contribute to the life in the cities. A well-designed building that uses natural materials, passive heating and cooling systems can not only make the life of its inhabitants healthier, but also contribute to preserving the environment and natural resources. This course aims to teach the fundamentals of sustainable and energy efficient building design, by focusing onBuilding envelopes Building materials and properties Building systems and operations (HVAC, lighting, water supply, sewage, garbage disposal, recycling and composting)Clean & renewable energy in buildingsRainwater harvestingWater and energy conservationEnergy modeling and performance evaluation of buildingsSmart buildings (Sensing and control systems)Net Zero buildings, Passive house standardsBuilding Rating systems (LEED, BREEAM, IGBC etc)

title: Design for Sustainable Development and Urbanization

department.name: Centre for Urban Science and Engineering

description: The course will teach design for sustainable development goals through the following ways: Analysis of the current design of SDG issues such as urban design, climate resilience, nutrition, education natural resource management (NRM,) through select examples drawn from national flagship programs with relevant international cases in terms of their objectives, and targets and their contribution to quality of life indicators. Underscoring the inter-dependencies of SDGs and identifying critical inter-sectoral spaces Understanding the influence of critical factors such as political economy, technology, culture, local contexts and their implications for the designing of SDG processes. Understanding conflict and contestations, and adversarial interests that impinge on SDG design Identifying strategic alternatives generated by diverse contexts Developing an Ecosystem approach to SDG by factoring the interplay of markets, governance, and society Developing integrated design solutions for SDGs Developing quality of life indicators Pedagogy: The course will use a pedagogy based on case studies and dialogues with different stakeholders for building comparative perspectives and design alternatives. It will apply creative methods of design education to SDGs that combines conceptual thinking with experiential and hands- on learning, theoretical constructs with participatory learning processes Pedagogic Process: Deconstruct: Bottom up approach to gathering and analysing Data Narratives Images Text Drawing concerns from issues Construct: Participatory methods of generating consensus on contested issues Interrogation Inference Framework of the problem and the solution Design System design Identifying issues, identity, structure, agency Mapping stakeholders and their stakes Understanding interdependent relationships Re-designing the framework for SDGs Transforming issues into strategies Revise Impact evaluation Policy dynamics Re-design for SDG issues

Themes such as water, waste management, transportation, environmental, social and physical infrastructures, demography, land-use planning The lab exercise will heavily base on data analytics, projection methods and policy inputs Students will be encouraged to interact with practitioners and city administration officers

title: Urban Planning in India: Theory, Challenges and Approaches

department.name: Centre for Urban Science and Engineering

description: This course provides a critical overview of the theory and practice of urban planning, the historical underpinnings of its disciplinary knowledge, the urban challenges in the Indian context and planning’s responses. The perspective of the course is critical and technical. It will illuminate the explicit and tacit knowledge underlying the practice of urban planning in India. Through a case study approach, students will understand the diversity of urban planning practices, as well as the challenges faced in provision of urban infrastructural services at different scales. Through actual site visits and small problem-solving exercises, students will explore sectoral interconnectedness and gaps. The course is also designed to enable students to think through urban challenges using multipronged strategic interventions. 9. Topics covered:  History of urban planning (international and in India)  Planning Disciplinary Knowledge (theory and technical knowledge)  Urban Challenges in the Indian city (informality, poverty, infrastructural gaps)  Planning Responses: Case studies of cities from India and the Global South (Curitiba, Goa, Pune etc.)  Future of Indian cities: sustainability directions

title: Urban Space: Theories and Critical Perspectives

department.name: Centre for Urban Science and Engineering

description: Urban space is understood differently from different disciplinary and lived perspectives that may contradict each other in important ways. Practically, it is also shaped by the interplay, synergies and conflicts of these perspectives in policy, politics and popular practice. This course provides an introduction to key perspectives drawing on diverse foundational and contemporary debates across fields like urban planning, human geography, critical urban theory, and urban environmentalism. It unpacks the taken-for-grantedness of urban space by illuminating the underlying complexity of the processes through which social and spatial `forces` shape each other. Such understanding is an important basis for effective practice (e.g. urban and infrastructural design, or policy practice) as well as research across social, design, and policy domains. The course will be taught with a mix of theoretical lectures, case studies and guided classroom discussions. Topics covered: Urban space: objectivist vs. subjectivist constructs Modernist planning, rationality and the technicist approach to space Humanist critique of modernist planning The social production of space Gender and space Environmentalism and urban space

title: Visual Design II
description: Application of design methods to complex communication design tasks. The emphasis is on planned design projects in different visual areas and appropriate application of design methods.Visual Design methodology for various stages of design process 302226 research, analysis, ideation, concepts, prototyping and evaluation. - Using different methods for doing primary and secondary research, user studies and need finding. - Methods of data analysis, cross mappings, insights and problem identification.- Role of creativity, role playing, brain storming, body storming, metamorphic thinking and other methods of concept generation. - Prototyping and Evaluation Methodologies.- Presentation and documentation of the design task.The Vastu-sutra Upanishad methodology for generation of symbolic form: Setting intended meaning, Realization, Articulation of attributes, Mental visualization, Projection of the visualization on a semantic and hierarchic grid. Enhancement of the image. Form creation algorithm for the visualized image for its replication without loss.

title: Reproduction Methods and Technology
description: A study of printing technology. Introduction to all the major processes of printing. Influence of printing process on design. Discussions on the constraints and possibilities with each of the printing process. An outline of paper technology. Study of properties, finishes, features and uses of different types papers available in the country. Study of production methods of books, new papers, magazines etc. Study of packaging using different materials. Handling print projects through digital media. Digital format image manipulation, Colour correction, font management, colour proofing, pre flight checks. Innovations in print technology like C2P.

title: Art, Design and Society II
description: Key issues in visual arts (painting and installation art, photography, films), performing arts, public art. Concepts and concerns, and discourse on contemporary arts practice. The course involves talks by visiting artists, writers, film-makers, theatre artists as well as site visits to organizations and institutions that support arts-based activities and education.

title: Indian Thoughts and Tradition
description: Study of Indian thought and philosophy and its relation to visual communications. Meaning of our festivals, mythology, the nature of religious ceremonies and other cultural diversities. Study of various Indian visual symbols. Study of Indian patterns and colours. Discussion of Indian cultural identity and its interpretation through visual communications. Experiments to create design with inherent Indianess.

title: Semantics & Communication Theory
description: The course explores relationship between human information processing and design of messages. Introduction to the two brain theory, short term memory, chunking theory and their implications on human information processing. Human information processing strategies with emphasis on processing of visual information. Introduction to visual perception and Gestalt laws of organization. Introduction to information theory and their application to spatial and spatio-temporal message design. Concept of attention in perception. Relationship between message design and attention, supported by eye movement studies. Exploring relationships between the semantics and the structure messages. Course is supported by studio which uses these theories in development of visual messages.
